patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors
[PATCH v3] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_log_dev_io_error() function to log device I/O errors for all bio error status codes, not just BLK_STS_IOERR and BLK_STS_TARGET. The change is motivated by the observation that some block layer drivers return unexpected status values, leading to incorrect conclusions about system behavior.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with minor suggestions for improvement, specifically recommending the use of a switch statement to handle different error status codes more clearly.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Apr 06, 2026 at 09:15:15AM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> So handle the known expected errors and make some noise on the rest
> which we expect won't really happen.

Looks good:

Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>

although now that you have an unlikely annotation for the non-ok
slow patch I'd use a switch statement for clarity int the second
pass.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 5/5] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 5/5] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch limits the iterations of the `shrink_delalloc` function in Btrfs to prevent it from blocking indefinitely, especially when dealing with large amounts of writeback data. The function now iterates over delalloc work in units of 128MiB, rather than trying to reclaim all available space at once. This change aims to strike a balance between making progress on delalloc and not starving other types of reservations, which can lead to system stalls and long commit times.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch is generally good, but suggested moving a variable declaration inside the loop instead of keeping it at the top level.
review_comments.raw_body:
We can also move the 'items' declaration inside this loop instead of top level.

Otherwise:

Reviewed-by: Filipe Manana <fdmanana@suse.com>

Thanks.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 4/5] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 4/5] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the data type of the 'outstanding_extents' field in the Btrfs inode structure from an unsigned integer (u32) to a 64-bit unsigned integer (u64). This change is motivated by the fact that the maximum file size is limited by LLONG_MAX, which is approximately 2^63. As a result, the maximum extent size computation in Btrfs has always been bounded above by this value, making it necessary to use a larger data type to accurately represent this value. The patch also updates several functions and variables to work with the new u64 data type.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer requested the addition of extended assertions in the code to provide more informative error messages when certain conditions are not met.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please use extended assertions that print the checked values, so that
we can have useful output to debug if they fail.

Thanks.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 3/5] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/5] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation
[PATCH v2 3/5] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue where the btrfs kernel module under-reserved space for delayed reference reservations during delalloc writes when compression is enabled. The problem arises from an incorrect estimate of outstanding extents due to a mismatch between maximum uncompressed and compressed extent sizes. To fix this, the patch introduces new functions in btrfs_inode.h to calculate the maximum extent size based on inode properties and uses these values to accurately reserve space for delayed references. As a result, the patch improves the accuracy of delalloc reservation calculations and prevents under-reservation issues even when compression is forced or always enabled for an inode.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Filipe Manana requested that the patch adhere to the kernel's coding style, specifically capitalizing the first word of each sentence and ensuring sentences are punctuated.

The reviewer suggested making a function u64 btrfs_inode_max_extent_size() static because it is only used within inode.c and does not need to be exported.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please follow our coding style: the first word is capitalized and
sentence should finish with punctuation.

---

There's no need to export this, make it static since it's not used
outside inode.c.

Thanks.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 2/5] btrfs: account for csum delayed_refs in delalloc

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/5] btrfs: account for csum delayed_refs in delalloc
[PATCH v2 2/5] btrfs: account for csum delayed_refs in delalloc

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues in the Btrfs delalloc space management, specifically accounting for checksum delayed references during delalloc operations. The first patch in the series introduces a new function to calculate the reserved space for csum tree updates, while subsequent patches address additional concerns and edge cases.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Identified two missing cases for csum tree updates during delalloc operations: COWing blocks from subvolume trees and raid stripe trees. Suggested adding reservations for these cases, as well as skipping the reservation if writing into a nocow/nosum inode.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Apr 3, 2026 at 11:29 PM Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io> wrote:
>
> As ordered_extents complete, they not only produce direct delayed refs,
> they also add csums via add_pending_csums. This produces some number of
> metadata delayed_refs as the csum tree is cow-ed. These refs are counted
> against the trans_handle delayed_rsv, thanks to trans->adding_csums.
>
> As a result, just like we account for the extent tree and free space
> tree when reserving delayed_refs for delalloc, we must also reserve for
> the csum tree. This is mirrored by the non-delayed-ref metadata
> reservation already accounting for csums.
>
> This serves to ensure we have a proper worst case estimate for
> delayed_rsv from delalloc.
>
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> ---
>  fs/btrfs/delalloc-space.c | 13 ++++++++++---
>  1 file changed, 10 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/delalloc-space.c b/fs/btrfs/delalloc-space.c
> index a087be55409f..c2e5c7758ed5 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/delalloc-space.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/delalloc-space.c
> @@ -252,12 +252,19 @@ static void btrfs_inode_rsv_release(struct btrfs_inode *inode, bool qgroup_free)
>
>  /*
>   * Each delalloc extent could become an ordered_extent and end up inserting a
> - * new extent into the extent and free space trees. So we must reserve
> - * delayed ref space up front for that.
> + * new extent into the extent and free space trees. It also may end up writing
> + * csums, so we must reserve delayed ref space up front for all of that.

Ok, this patch addresses my comment in the previous patch regarding
the missing accounting for csum tree updates.

But it lacks accounting for new delayed refs when COWing blocks from:

1) A subvolume tree (insert a new file extent item, or modify an
existing one in case of prealloc writes).

2) Raid stripe tree.


>   */
>  static u64 delalloc_calc_delayed_refs_rsv(const struct btrfs_fs_info *fs_info, u64 nr_extents)
>  {
> -       return btrfs_calc_delayed_ref_bytes(fs_info, nr_extents);
> +       /*
> +        * btrfs_calc_delayed_ref_bytes() does not assume csums as it is
> +        * also called from metadata writing contexts. So the extra
> +        * metadata insertion reservation represents the possibility of
> +        * delayed refs during csum tree writes.
> +        */
> +       return btrfs_calc_delayed_ref_bytes(fs_info, nr_extents) +
> +               btrfs_calc_insert_metadata_size(fs_info, nr_extents);

We can also skip the reservation for the csum tree if we are writing
into a nocow/nosum inode.
Check the rest of delalloc-space.c: there's an optimization I did
years ago to reduce space reservations in case the inode has the
BTRFS_INODE_NODATASUM flag set.

Thanks.

>  }
>
>  static void btrfs_calculate_inode_block_rsv_size(struct btrfs_fs_info *fs_info,
> --
> 2.53.0
>
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc
[PATCH v2 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a problem where the Btrfs file system does not reserve enough space for delayed references (delayed_refs) during delalloc operations. Delalloc uses a per-inode block reservation to anticipate metadata needs, but it does not account for delayed refs. As a result, when writeback occurs and delayed refs are generated, they draw from an under-reserved trans_handle's delayed_refs_rsv, leading to incorrect assessments of reserved space in the metadata space reclaim logic. The patch fixes this by reserving space for delayed refs during delalloc operations.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that delayed refs are created for multiple trees (extent, free space, checksum, and raid stripe) and requested accounting for nr_extents * 2 + additional trees

Reviewer Filipe Manana noted that the function's return type should be changed to void because none of its callers care about the return value.

Reviewer noted that writing into a prealloc extent can also generate delayed refs, which are not currently reserved in the delalloc logic.

Reviewer noted that the patch introduces a formatting inconsistency by using curly braces for single-statement ifs, which goes against the kernel's style preference.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ok, so that's 2 delayed refs, one to modify the extent tree and
another to modify the free space tree.
So below, techically we should account for nr_extents * 2.

And we actually need to modify more trees: the csum tree if we have
csums to insert and the new raid stripe tree.

---

Why return the number of bytes?
None of the callers cares about the return value, it can be just void.

---

So for the other case, writing into a prealloc extent, we should also
migrate space space because we will update the fs tree to modify a
file extent item (the call to btrfs_mark_extent_written()) and that
can generate delayed refs if we need to COW blocks from the fs tree
for that.

---

Another style change and this one goes against our style preference -
no { } for single statement if.

This is just a quick pass over the change, I'll look into the
following patches soon.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a minor issue with style changes in the original patch, promising to double-check the full patch output and undo any unnecessary modifications.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sorry for the leaked style changes. Those were places I modified in the
first try using a new block_rsv and I didn't properly undo them while
changing it to the new migration design. I should have double checked
the full patch output for silly stuff like that.

Thanks for the review,
Boris

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/5] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/5] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback
[PATCH v2 0/5] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses stalls caused by sudden writeback in Btrfs file systems on large memory systems. The patches improve reservations for delayed refs and smooth out the work in flush_space() to prevent system stalling due to excessive ordered extents and delayed refs blocking metadata space system operations. The changes include updating rsv accounting, cleaning up types counting max extents, reducing the unit of work in shrink_delalloc(), and capping iterations to 128M. These modifications aim to reduce stalls on large systems with high memory and disk capacity.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer pointed out that the patch should account for two delayed refs per extent, one for modifying the extent tree and another for the free space tree, as well as additional trees such as the csum tree and raid stripe tree.

Reviewer Filipe Manana pointed out that the function should return void instead of an integer count because none of its callers rely on the return value.

Filipe Manana noted that writing into a prealloc extent can also generate delayed refs due to COW operations, and requested migration of space space in this case.

Reviewer Filipe Manana pointed out that the patch introduces a style inconsistency by using curly braces for single-statement if blocks, which goes against the kernel's coding style preference.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ok, so that's 2 delayed refs, one to modify the extent tree and
another to modify the free space tree.
So below, techically we should account for nr_extents * 2.

And we actually need to modify more trees: the csum tree if we have
csums to insert and the new raid stripe tree.

---

Why return the number of bytes?
None of the callers cares about the return value, it can be just void.

---

So for the other case, writing into a prealloc extent, we should also
migrate space space because we will update the fs tree to modify a
file extent item (the call to btrfs_mark_extent_written()) and that
can generate delayed refs if we need to COW blocks from the fs tree
for that.

---

Another style change and this one goes against our style preference -
no { } for single statement if.

This is just a quick pass over the change, I'll look into the
following patches soon.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the patch lacks accounting for delayed refs created when COWing blocks from subvolume and raid stripe trees, which was previously identified as an issue in another patch.

Reviewer Filipe Manana suggested that the patch could be improved by skipping the reservation for the csum tree when writing into a nocow/nosum inode, and also mentioned an existing optimization in delalloc-space.c that reduces space reservations for such inodes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ok, this patch addresses my comment in the previous patch regarding
the missing accounting for csum tree updates.

But it lacks accounting for new delayed refs when COWing blocks from:

1) A subvolume tree (insert a new file extent item, or modify an
existing one in case of prealloc writes).

2) Raid stripe tree.

---

We can also skip the reservation for the csum tree if we are writing
into a nocow/nosum inode.
Check the rest of delalloc-space.c: there's an optimization I did
years ago to reduce space reservations in case the inode has the
BTRFS_INODE_NODATASUM flag set.

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a minor issue with style changes in the patch, promising to be more careful in the future.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sorry for the leaked style changes. Those were places I modified in the
first try using a new block_rsv and I didn't properly undo them while
changing it to the new migration design. I should have double checked
the full patch output for silly stuff like that.

Thanks for the review,
Boris

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Filipe Manana pointed out that the patch description does not conform to the kernel's coding style, specifically stating that the first word of each sentence should be capitalized and sentences should end with punctuation.

Reviewer Filipe Manana noted that the function in question is only used within inode.c and suggested making its declaration static to prevent unnecessary exports.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please follow our coding style: the first word is capitalized and
sentence should finish with punctuation.

---

There's no need to export this, make it static since it's not used
outside inode.c.

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Filipe Manana requested the patch author to add extended assertions that print the checked values for easier debugging when they fail.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please use extended assertions that print the checked values, so that
we can have useful output to debug if they fail.

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested a minor improvement to code organization by moving the 'items' declaration inside a loop, rather than having it at the top level.
review_comments.raw_body:
We can also move the 'items' declaration inside this loop instead of top level.

Otherwise:

Reviewed-by: Filipe Manana <fdmanana@suse.com>

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc
Re: [PATCH v2] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors
Re: [PATCH v2 16/20] vfio/cxl: Register regions with VFIO layer
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch introduces a new VFIO layer for registering regions with the CXL (Coherent Accelerator Processor Interface) protocol, allowing for cache-coherent memory access between host and guest.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Expressed agreement with the idea of marking reserved memory in firmware, but questioned the need for a specific method (hotplug, private node, memremap) to achieve cache-coherent access.

review_comments.author: Jason Gunthorpe
review_comments.summary: Proposed using the request_resource mechanism to lock CXL ranges in the resource tree, and suggested turning phys_addr_t's into large cachable special PTEs inside a VMA.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Apr 06, 2026 at 05:22:06PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Sat, Apr 04, 2026 at 12:36:53PM -0700, Dan Williams wrote:
> > Jason Gunthorpe wrote:
> > > 
> > > The entire thing needs to be mmapable in a cache coherent way since
> > > that is what the HW semantic is. If you try to do something else you
> > > will break KVM support since it follows the VMA.
> > 
> > Then I assume it matters that memremap() sometimes silently falls back
> > to the direct map. The "VFIO owns" expectation needs to guard against
> > some helpful platform firmware mapping accelerator memory as System RAM.
> > 
> > At a minimum having VFIO fail to map in that case helps with the
> > argument I have been making that "no, EFI_CONVENTIONAL_MEMORY type +
> > EFI_SPECIFIC_PURPOSE flag" is not suitable for accelerators with private
> > CXL memory. Those want to be enforcing "EFI_RESERVED".
> 
> Agree - in fact I would argue any potential user of private nodes should
> have some kind of splat saying the memory should be marked reserved in
> the first place, otherwise it's a firmware bug.

I would expect this to happen via the request_resource mechanism, if
MM is using the CXL range it should be locked in the resource tree and
vfio should request it and then fail during the initial startup
phases.

> I need to read up a little bit on this area, but i don't see the "needs
> to be mmap'able in a cache coherent way" to be an argument for one
> particular method or another (hotplug, private node, memremap, etc).

It is speaking to what VFIO has to do. It is the exclusive owner of
the physical range, it does not have struct pages, it must be cachable
for VFIO and KVM to work - not alot of choices here. Turn the
phys_addr_t's into large cachable special PTEs inside a VMA.

Jason

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] dcache: warn when a dentry is freed with a non-empty ->d_lru

version_history.subject: [PATCH] dcache: warn when a dentry is freed with a non-empty ->d_lru

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a warning to the Linux kernel when a dentry (directory entry) is freed while its 'd_lru' list is not empty. This is done to prevent use-after-free errors, which can cause panics in the system. The problem was identified through a series of panics on hosts with high process churn and was also reported by syzbot. The patch adds a WARN_ON_ONCE() call to the dentry_free function to catch this issue and make it easier to debug.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/4] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/4] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] mm: fix IOCB_DONTCACHE write performance with rate-limited writeback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses performance issues caused by the IOCB_DONTCACHE flag, which triggers aggressive flushing of dirty pages on every write operation. The problem arises when multiple writers contend for writeback submission, leading to severe serialization and reduced throughput. To fix this, a new function filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() is introduced, which uses two rate-limiting mechanisms: skip-if-busy (checking if writeback is already in progress) and proportional cap (limiting the number of pages flushed). These mechanisms prevent excessive flushing and contention between concurrent writers. The patch replaces the existing filemap_flush_range() call with the new function in generic_write_sync().

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Christoph Hellwig questioned the connection between the patch's first and second paragraphs, expressing uncertainty about the relevance of the initial explanation to the proposed solution.

Reviewer Christoph Hellwig questioned the necessity of the patch, pointing out that filemap_flush_range and filemap_writeback already cap the number of written pages to the range passed in, making the proposed change unclear.

Reviewer noted that the new filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() function, introduced to fix IOCB_DONTCACHE write performance issues, has an incorrect number of arguments compared to the existing filemap_writeback() function.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm not sure the first how you think the first paragraph relate to
the second.

---

This doesn't make any sense at all.
filemap_flush_range/filemap_writeback always caps the number of written
pages to the range passed in.  What do you think is the change here?

---

filemap_writeback only has 5 arguments in any tree I've looked at
including linux-next.

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Ritesh suspects that the performance penalty in a single sequential writer is due to contention between the submission side and completion side on the xa_lock, which is exacerbated by IOCB_DONTCACHE's behavior of skipping flushes and allowing more dirty pages to be written before completion. He suggests adding a perf callgraph to visualize this contention.

The reviewer noted that the new filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() function may not correctly handle cases where a subsequent writer does not write to the same range as the previous writer, potentially leaving dirty pages unflushed until background writeback occurs.

Reviewer Ritesh questioned whether the patch was properly rebased and pointed out an error in the filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() function call, specifically mentioning a potentially incorrect argument
review_comments.raw_body:
I am not sure if this 2.5x performance penalty in a "single" sequential
writer is due to throttling logic. On giving it some thoughts, I suspect
if this is because, the submission side and the completion side both
takes the xa_lock and hence could be contending on that.

For e.g. since this patch skips doing the flush the second time, (note
that writeback is active when the same writer dirtied the page during
previous write), this allows the writer to do more work of writing data
to page cache pages, instead of waiting on the xa_lock which the
completion callback could be holding (folio_end_writeback() -> folio_end_dropbehind())

If I see Peak Dirty data from the link you shared [1] in single writer case...

Mode                    MB/s	p50 (ms)	p99 (ms)	p99.9 (ms)	Peak Dirty	Peak Cache
dontcache (unpatched)	1179	3.2	    103.3	    170.9	    14 MB	    4.7 GB
dontcache (patched)	1453	5.4	    43.8	    57.4	    36 GB	    45 GB

... this too shows that the submission side is writing more dirty pages,
then the completion side able to write it... 

I suspect this contention (between submission and completion) could more
in IOCB_DONTCACHE case, since the completion side also removes the folio
from the page cache within the same xa_lock, which is not the same with
normal buffered writes.

Maybe a perf callgraph showing the contention would be nicer thing to add
here [1] ;). 

[1]: https://markdownpastebin.com/?id=96249deb897a401ba32acbce05312dcc

---

Yes, but the next writer may not write the dirty pages, of the previous
writer which skipped the flush call right (even if it finds the tag
clear)? Because filemap_dontcache_writeback_range( ) passes the range
and nr_to_write that means, unless the previous writer dirtied the same
range, the new writer won't be able to write the dirty pages of the
previous writer correct? So, it is mainly only the background writeback
now, which will flush this dirty pages of the writer which skipped the
flush (unless of course a fsync/sync call is made).

But having said that, I agree, this patch series is a nice performance
improvement overall :)

---

Was this rebased against some other tree? I couldn't find it in
linux-next. I think, that last argument is wrong.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged a reviewer's point about potential contention for xa_lock, agreed to investigate further using perf tools.

The author acknowledged a concern about the effectiveness of relying on VM dirty limits when DONTCACHE writers skip flushing and explained that they tried an alternative approach but it performed worse, indicating no immediate plan to revisit this issue.

Author acknowledged that their previous patch contained an error, promised to fix it in a revised version (v2) and will resubmit soon.
review_comments.raw_body:
That's an interesting point.

The theory I've been operating on is that the flusher thread ends up
squatting on the xa_lock for a while when memory gets tight, and that
blocks other readers and writers. Staying ahead of the dirty limits and
limiting the amount of flush work that each writer does alleviates
contention for that lock and that's what improves the performance.

You're right though. I'll plan to play around with perf and see if I
can confirm the theory.

---

Correct. When DONTCACHE writers end up skipping the flush, we rely on
VM dirty limits to eventually take care of flushing the data that got
skipped. That's why the DONTCACHE dirty pagecache max size ends up
looking close to buffered mode's.

I did play with a patch that had the writers attempt to flush 4x more
than they had written when memory was tight to compensate for that, but
it ended up performing worse than this set. It's possible that tuning
that down to 2x or so would do better, but I decided to just stop here
and post what I had.

---

Yes, my apologies. I think this must have been a bad merge on my part
during the rebase. I'll post a v2 in the near future.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledges a concern about writeback submission contention between concurrent writers and agrees to verify it using perf.

Author acknowledged that their initial results may have been incorrect, promising to recheck the issue and provide further feedback.

Author acknowledged a mistake in their previous patch and plans to fix it by reverting to a corrected version from their 'dontcache' branch.
review_comments.raw_body:
The belief is that under heavy parallel write workload on the same
inode, the writers all end up stacking up on the mapping's xa_lock.
However as Ritesh points out, I should probably confirm that with perf.

---

I had some earlier results that indicated that this did help. It's
possible they were bogus though. I'll recheck that and get back to you.

---

I think this was a bad merge on my part. Mea culpa. The version in the
"dontcache" branch of my tree should be correct.

Thanks for the review!
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch's performance improvement is relatively small (~23%) in a single sequential write case, and requested further analysis of this specific scenario.

Reviewer Ritesh noted that the improvement in throughput from 1179 MB/s to 1453 MB/s can be attributed to reduced contention on xa_lock, specifically for single-write sequential testcases.

Reviewer Ritesh noted that the patch does not address the issue of concurrent writers causing serialization on the writeback submission path when using IOCB_DONTCACHE, and suggested that the patch should be modified to handle this scenario.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sorry my bad.. I mis-understood this 2.5x delta at first.

So in a single sequential write case, what this patch is mainly
improving is from unpatched RWF_DONTCACHE (1179 MB/s) to patched
RWF_DONTCACHE (1453 MB/s) = ~23% improvement.

So the below theory which I was talking about was from this delta
perspective i.e. comparing unpatched v/s patched RWF_DONTCACHE mode.

---

That's right for comparison between buffered write against RWF_DONTCACHE.
But what I meant in above was for the improvement from 1179 MB/s to 1453
MB/s could be accounted to less contention on xa_lock on patched version
v/s unpatched version for single write sequential testcase.

---

Yes, thanks, that will be nice to have!

-ritesh

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that setting both .range_start and .range_end in writeback_iter() loop, combined with non-zero nr_to_write, could lead to unexpected behavior where all pages in the range are written instead of just the specified number.
review_comments.raw_body:
But nr_to_write should not change anything.  If .range_start and
.range_end are set in a writeback_iter() loop, writeback_iter will try to
get and writeback every page in the range.  Setting nr_to_write in
addition to that could only reduce the amount written if it was less than
the size of the range, which in your patch it isn't.

In fact we should probably have a debug check to never set both a range
and nr_to_write as that combination doesn't make sense.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: add atomic flush guard for IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds an atomic flush guard to prevent a 'thundering herd' problem that causes high tail latency when multiple IOCB_DONTCACHE writers submit flushes simultaneously after the PAGECACHE_TAG_WRITEBACK tag clears. The patch introduces an AS_DONTCACHE_FLUSHING flag and uses test_and_set_bit() to ensure only one writer flushes at a time, while also adding a dirty pressure escape hatch that forces extra flushes when dirty pages exceed 75% of the dirty_ratio threshold.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Christoph Hellwig expressed concern about a potential lock ordering issue and suggested alternative approaches to handling IOCB_DONTCACHE writes, including disabling in-caller writeback or queuing up wb_writeback_work instances.
review_comments.raw_body:
This sounds like a bad reimplementation of the single writeback thread
:)

Have you considered stopping to do in-caller writeback for
IOCB_DONTCACHE vs just leaving it to the writeback daeon?

Either by totally disabling the writeback and just leaving the
dropbehind bit, or by queuing up wb_writeback_work instances for
the ranges, or by just increasing the pressure for the writeback
daemon.  Note that with all schemes including the one in this patch
we might eventually run into writeback scalability limits, which
will require multiple writeback workers.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the current implementation of 'dropbehind' mode is not sufficient to address tail latency issues, and plans to explore a new approach where each writer submits writeback work without lock contention, potentially benefiting both IOCB_DONTCACHE and normal buffered writes.
review_comments.raw_body:
I did test a "dropbehind" mode that just set the dropbehind bit without
doing the flush at the end of the write. It was better than stock
dontcache but the tail latencies were still pretty bad.

I think having each writer do some writeback submission work makes a
lot of sense. It helps keep the dirty pages below the dirty thresholds
and doesn't seem to tax each writing task _too_ much. The trick is
avoiding lock contention while doing it.

I think what would be ideal would be to have some (lockless) mechanism
to say "there is enough data touched by the range just written to kick
off a write that's a suitable size for the backing store". Each writer
could check that and then kick off writeback for an approprite range.

I think this even could be beneficial in the normal buffered write
codepath too.

Anyway, I'll play around with this idea some more and come back with a
v2.

Thanks for the review!
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch introduces shared resource contention between IOCB_DONTCACHE writers, which can lead to scalability issues, and suggested avoiding such contention by using a single thread for writeback operations.

The reviewer suggested queuing up writeback on the writeback thread for each dontcache write and proposed an initial approximation of queuing a wb_writeback_work for each range, but recommended finding a more efficient way to increase a threshold instead.

Reviewer noted that the 30-second timeout for writeback can be actively harmful, especially on SSDs and HDDs, and suggested considering a shorter or dynamic timeout.
review_comments.raw_body:
Well, an any time you hit a shared resources from multiple threads you
create that lock contention.   Which is why in file system and writeback
land we've moved away from random user processes hitting both data and
metadata (e.g. XFS AIL) writeback as it leads to these scalability
issues.  At some point we might run out of steam in a single thread,
although so far that's mostly been because it does stupid things
(e.g. writeback on file systems doing complex allocator stuff).

---

And that is called the writeback thread.  So what we should do there
is to make sure we queue up writeback on it for each dontcache write.
Initially queuing up a wb_writeback_work for each range might be first
approximation, although we should probably find a way to just increase
a threshold if going down that road.

---

Yes, we've had lots of observation that the current 30s timeout is
actively harmful.  Especially on SSDs, but even on HDD just keeping
the active might make sense.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author is addressing Christoph Hellwig's suggestion to wake up flusher threads after every RWF_DONTCACHE write, and considering whether to extend wb_writeback_work() to allow for doing work on a range within a single inode.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ok, I like that idea. I'll give that a shot and see how it does. I'll
note that there is no way to specify an inode or range (yet) in
wb_writeback_work().

Do you think it's sufficient to just call something like
wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi() after every RWF_DONTCACHE write, or should
I extend wb_writeback_work to allow for doing work on a range within a
single inode?

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that IOCB_DONTCACHE writes don't require integrity operations and suggested waking the flusher thread instead of implementing atomic flush guard, also requesting a way to communicate higher writeback targets.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't think we care about what exact data is written back as this is
not an integrity operation.  So just waking the flusher thread sounds
like the right thing, but we might also need a way to communicate
the higher writeback target.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] testing: add nfsd-io-bench NFS server benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a benchmark suite called nfsd-io-bench to test the performance of NFSD I/O modes using fio and the libnfs backend against an NFS server on localhost. The suite includes tests for buffered, don'tcache, and direct I/O modes via NFSD debugfs controls. It also includes scripts to orchestrate the test matrix, parse results from fio JSON output, and configure the NFS export for testing. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive benchmarking tool for evaluating NFSD I/O performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] testing: add dontcache-bench local filesystem benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite called 'dontcache-bench' to test the IOCB_DONTCACHE flag on local filesystems using fio's io_uring engine. The suite includes various job files and scripts to run and parse the results of these tests, mirroring the existing nfsd-io-bench test matrix but with different I/O modes. The goal is to evaluate the performance impact of disabling caching for I/O operations on local filesystems.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patchset improves write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag by addressing flush contention issues that occur during heavy write activity. The patches modify the Linux kernel's mm module to alleviate this contention, resulting in better performance for writes. The changes were tested with various workloads and IO modes, including buffered, direct, and unpatched dontcache, and showed significant improvements over the original RWF_DONTCACHE implementation. The patchset also includes benchmarking scripts to demonstrate the results of these tests.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Christoph Hellwig expressed confusion about the connection between the first and second paragraphs of the patch description, but did not provide any specific technical feedback or suggestions.

Christoph Hellwig questioned the purpose of the patch, pointing out that filemap_flush_range and filemap_writeback already cap the number of written pages to the range passed in, and requested clarification on what change this patch is intended to make.

Reviewer Christoph Hellwig noted that filemap_writeback() has only 5 arguments across all trees, including linux-next, and requested an update to match the patch's 6-argument version.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm not sure the first how you think the first paragraph relate to
the second.

---

This doesn't make any sense at all.
filemap_flush_range/filemap_writeback always caps the number of written
pages to the range passed in.  What do you think is the change here?

---

filemap_writeback only has 5 arguments in any tree I've looked at
including linux-next.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig expressed strong disagreement with the current implementation of IOCB_DONTCACHE write performance improvement, suggesting that it is a bad reimplementation of the single writeback thread and proposing alternative approaches to handle writeback for this flag.
review_comments.raw_body:
This sounds like a bad reimplementation of the single writeback thread
:)

Have you considered stopping to do in-caller writeback for
IOCB_DONTCACHE vs just leaving it to the writeback daeon?

Either by totally disabling the writeback and just leaving the
dropbehind bit, or by queuing up wb_writeback_work instances for
the ranges, or by just increasing the pressure for the writeback
daemon.  Note that with all schemes including the one in this patch
we might eventually run into writeback scalability limits, which
will require multiple writeback workers.

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Ritesh suspects that the 2.5x performance penalty in sequential writes is due to contention between the submission side and completion side taking the xa_lock, which allows the writer to do more work but causes a bottleneck on the completion callback holding folio_end_dropbehind().

The reviewer noted that filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() may not write the dirty pages of the previous writer if it skipped the flush call, and instead relies on background writeback to flush these pages unless a fsync/sync call is made.

Reviewer Ritesh questioned the patch's rebase status and pointed out an incorrect argument in one of the patches.
review_comments.raw_body:
I am not sure if this 2.5x performance penalty in a "single" sequential
writer is due to throttling logic. On giving it some thoughts, I suspect
if this is because, the submission side and the completion side both
takes the xa_lock and hence could be contending on that.

For e.g. since this patch skips doing the flush the second time, (note
that writeback is active when the same writer dirtied the page during
previous write), this allows the writer to do more work of writing data
to page cache pages, instead of waiting on the xa_lock which the
completion callback could be holding (folio_end_writeback() -> folio_end_dropbehind())

If I see Peak Dirty data from the link you shared [1] in single writer case...

Mode                    MB/s	p50 (ms)	p99 (ms)	p99.9 (ms)	Peak Dirty	Peak Cache
dontcache (unpatched)	1179	3.2	    103.3	    170.9	    14 MB	    4.7 GB
dontcache (patched)	1453	5.4	    43.8	    57.4	    36 GB	    45 GB

... this too shows that the submission side is writing more dirty pages,
then the completion side able to write it... 

I suspect this contention (between submission and completion) could more
in IOCB_DONTCACHE case, since the completion side also removes the folio
from the page cache within the same xa_lock, which is not the same with
normal buffered writes.

Maybe a perf callgraph showing the contention would be nicer thing to add
here [1] ;). 

[1]: https://markdownpastebin.com/?id=96249deb897a401ba32acbce05312dcc

---

Yes, but the next writer may not write the dirty pages, of the previous
writer which skipped the flush call right (even if it finds the tag
clear)? Because filemap_dontcache_writeback_range( ) passes the range
and nr_to_write that means, unless the previous writer dirtied the same
range, the new writer won't be able to write the dirty pages of the
previous writer correct? So, it is mainly only the background writeback
now, which will flush this dirty pages of the writer which skipped the
flush (unless of course a fsync/sync call is made).

But having said that, I agree, this patch series is a nice performance
improvement overall :)

---

Was this rebased against some other tree? I couldn't find it in
linux-next. I think, that last argument is wrong.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged a reviewer's point about flusher thread contention on xa_lock, agreed to test their theory using perf, and plans to re-evaluate performance improvement.

The author acknowledged a concern about the performance of RWF_DONTCACHE with write-heavy workloads and explained that the patch relies on VM dirty limits to flush skipped data, but did not commit to further tuning or revisions.

Author acknowledged that there was an issue with the patchset, attributed it to a bad merge during rebase, and promised to post a revised version (v2) soon.
sentiment_signals: did not commit to further revisions
review_comments.raw_body:
That's an interesting point.

The theory I've been operating on is that the flusher thread ends up
squatting on the xa_lock for a while when memory gets tight, and that
blocks other readers and writers. Staying ahead of the dirty limits and
limiting the amount of flush work that each writer does alleviates
contention for that lock and that's what improves the performance.

You're right though. I'll plan to play around with perf and see if I
can confirm the theory.

---

Correct. When DONTCACHE writers end up skipping the flush, we rely on
VM dirty limits to eventually take care of flushing the data that got
skipped. That's why the DONTCACHE dirty pagecache max size ends up
looking close to buffered mode's.

I did play with a patch that had the writers attempt to flush 4x more
than they had written when memory was tight to compensate for that, but
it ended up performing worse than this set. It's possible that tuning
that down to 2x or so would do better, but I decided to just stop here
and post what I had.

---

Yes, my apologies. I think this must have been a bad merge on my part
during the rebase. I'll post a v2 in the near future.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges a concern about parallel write workloads causing writers to stack up on the mapping's xa_lock and agrees to verify this with perf.

Author acknowledges that initial results may have been incorrect, plans to recheck and provide further information.

Author acknowledges a mistake was made during merge and promises to fix it by reverting to a previous version from their tree.
review_comments.raw_body:
The belief is that under heavy parallel write workload on the same
inode, the writers all end up stacking up on the mapping's xa_lock.
However as Ritesh points out, I should probably confirm that with perf.

---

I had some earlier results that indicated that this did help. It's
possible they were bogus though. I'll recheck that and get back to you.

---

I think this was a bad merge on my part. Mea culpa. The version in the
"dontcache" branch of my tree should be correct.

Thanks for the review!
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that RWF_DONTCACHE still has performance issues, particularly in tail latencies, and plans to restructure the patch in v2 to address this by having each writer submit writeback work without lock contention.
review_comments.raw_body:
I did test a "dropbehind" mode that just set the dropbehind bit without
doing the flush at the end of the write. It was better than stock
dontcache but the tail latencies were still pretty bad.

I think having each writer do some writeback submission work makes a
lot of sense. It helps keep the dirty pages below the dirty thresholds
and doesn't seem to tax each writing task _too_ much. The trick is
avoiding lock contention while doing it.

I think what would be ideal would be to have some (lockless) mechanism
to say "there is enough data touched by the range just written to kick
off a write that's a suitable size for the backing store". Each writer
could check that and then kick off writeback for an approprite range.

I think this even could be beneficial in the normal buffered write
codepath too.

Anyway, I'll play around with this idea some more and come back with a
v2.

Thanks for the review!
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer initially misinterpreted the patch's performance improvement, but upon reevaluation, they noted a 23% increase in sequential write speed from unpatched to patched RWF_DONTCACHE mode.

Reviewer Ritesh notes that the improvement in write performance from 1179 MB/s to 1453 MB/s can be attributed to reduced contention on xa_lock, specifically for single-write sequential testcases.

Reviewer Ritesh expressed a positive sentiment towards the patchset, stating that it would be 'nice to have' the improvements.
sentiment_signals: no specific technical concerns or suggestions
review_comments.raw_body:
Sorry my bad.. I mis-understood this 2.5x delta at first.

So in a single sequential write case, what this patch is mainly
improving is from unpatched RWF_DONTCACHE (1179 MB/s) to patched
RWF_DONTCACHE (1453 MB/s) = ~23% improvement.

So the below theory which I was talking about was from this delta
perspective i.e. comparing unpatched v/s patched RWF_DONTCACHE mode.

---

That's right for comparison between buffered write against RWF_DONTCACHE.
But what I meant in above was for the improvement from 1179 MB/s to 1453
MB/s could be accounted to less contention on xa_lock on patched version
v/s unpatched version for single write sequential testcase.

---

Yes, thanks, that will be nice to have!

-ritesh

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig noted that setting nr_to_write in addition to .range_start and .range_end in writeback_iter() can lead to writing every page in the range, which is unnecessary if nr_to_write is set to a value less than the range size. He suggested adding a debug check to prevent this combination.
review_comments.raw_body:
But nr_to_write should not change anything.  If .range_start and
.range_end are set in a writeback_iter() loop, writeback_iter will try to
get and writeback every page in the range.  Setting nr_to_write in
addition to that could only reduce the amount written if it was less than
the size of the range, which in your patch it isn't.

In fact we should probably have a debug check to never set both a range
and nr_to_write as that combination doesn't make sense.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary:
Christoph Hellwig noted that the patch introduces lock contention between multiple threads accessing shared resources, which can lead to scalability issues, and suggested that this is a problem that has been addressed in other areas of the kernel by separating data and metadata writeback.

Christoph Hellwig noted that the writeback thread is not properly handling dontcache writes and suggested queuing up writeback work for each range, possibly by increasing a threshold instead of creating a new wb_writeback_work for each range

Reviewer noted that the 30s timeout for IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback is actively harmful, especially on SSDs and HDDs, and suggested reconsidering the current timeout value.
review_comments.raw_body:
Well, an any time you hit a shared resources from multiple threads you
create that lock contention.   Which is why in file system and writeback
land we've moved away from random user processes hitting both data and
metadata (e.g. XFS AIL) writeback as it leads to these scalability
issues.  At some point we might run out of steam in a single thread,
although so far that's mostly been because it does stupid things
(e.g. writeback on file systems doing complex allocator stuff).

---

And that is called the writeback thread.  So what we should do there
is to make sure we queue up writeback on it for each dontcache write.
Initially queuing up a wb_writeback_work for each range might be first
approximation, although we should probably find a way to just increase
a threshold if going down that road.

---

Yes, we've had lots of observation that the current 30s timeout is
actively harmful.  Especially on SSDs, but even on HDD just keeping
the active might make sense.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author is addressing Christoph Hellwig's suggestion to improve the patch by adding support for waking up flusher threads after RWF_DONTCACHE writes. The author agrees to try this approach and will note that it currently lacks inode or range specification in wb_writeback_work().
review_comments.raw_body:
Ok, I like that idea. I'll give that a shot and see how it does. I'll
note that there is no way to specify an inode or range (yet) in
wb_writeback_work().

Do you think it's sufficient to just call something like
wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi() after every RWF_DONTCACHE write, or should
I extend wb_writeback_work to allow for doing work on a range within a
single inode?

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that RWF_DONTCACHE is not an integrity operation and suggested waking the flusher thread instead of writing back exact data, also requesting a way to communicate the higher writeback target.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't think we care about what exact data is written back as this is
not an integrity operation.  So just waking the flusher thread sounds
like the right thing, but we might also need a way to communicate
the higher writeback target.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] nfsd: fix file change detection in CB_GETATTR
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue where file change detection was not working correctly in the NFS daemon's (nfsd) CB_GETATTR operation, which is used to retrieve attributes of a file. The problem was likely due to incorrect handling of buffered data on the client side. To fix this, the patch may involve adding a new attribute or modifying existing behavior to ensure accurate change detection.

review_comments.author: Scott Mayhew
review_comments.summary: reviewer questioned why the existing change attribute doesn't already account for pending buffered data, pointing out that dirty pages/folios are tracked on the nfs_inode and an elevated change attribute is sent in CB_GETATTR replies
review_comments.raw_body:
I guess I don't understand why the client's usage of the change
attribute doesn't already cover that.

If an application calls write() but not fsync(), close(), etc, and the
background flushers haven't kicked in yet, then the dirty pages/folios
will be tracked on the nfs_inode and the client will send the elevated
change attribute in the CB_GETATTR reply.

What am I missing?

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] nfsd: fix file change detection in CB_GETATTR
Re: [PATCH 1/1] nfsd: update mtime/ctime on CLONE and COPY in presence of delegated attributes
Re: [PATCH v2] nfsd: fix file change detection in CB_GETATTR
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue where file change detection was not working correctly in the NFS daemon's (nfsd) CB_GETATTR operation, which is used to retrieve attributes of a file. The problem was likely due to incorrect handling of buffered data on the client side. To fix this, the patch may involve adding a new attribute or modifying existing behavior to ensure accurate change detection.

review_comments.author: Scott Mayhew
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Scott Mayhew pointed out that the pseudocode in section 10.4.3 of the documentation only checks the change attribute to determine if a file has been modified, but then later mentions checking the file size, which is inconsistent and potentially misleading.

Reviewer Scott Mayhew pointed out that comparing the mtime is not specified in the spec and suggested that a different mtime from the server's would be a client bug, also expressing concern about crossing into delegated timestamps territory
review_comments.raw_body:
You're right, it's not.  I dislike how the pseudocode in 10.4.3 is only
checking the change attribute to determine if the file has been modified
and then 2 paragraphs down it talks about checking the file size.

At any rate, the main point of this fix is to make sure we're comparing
the size from the cb_getattr reply to the current size, not a cached
value.  For example:

client 1 does open(O_RDWR|O_CREATE_O_TRUNC)
client 1 writes some data and flushes it to the server
client 2 does some operation that triggers a CB_GETATTR

At this point client 1 doesn't have any modified data, so it sends the
original change attribute and its current file size (which should match
the current file size on the server).  With the current code, the server
compares the size client 1 sent with the value it cached on the
delegation (in this case 0).  The server treats it as if the client is
has modified data, which it does not.  That's all this patch is aiming
to fix.

---

But the spec doesn't say anything about comparing the mtime.  Plus, if
the client is sending the original change attribute but a different
mtime than what the server has, wouldn't that be a client bug?

Plus, wouldn't it sort of be crossing into delegated timestamps
territory if we started checking the mtime?

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author responded to feedback about relying on file size for change detection, explaining that the change attribute sent by the client is not a reliable indicator of buffered writes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Not really. The change attr sent by the client is "special". It's
usually just original change attr at time of delegation +1 iff
something has been modified. I don't see that as a reliable indicator
that there is still buffered write data on the client.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author responded to feedback about relying on file size for change detection, suggesting extending the NFS spec by adding an attribute that indicates buffered data.
review_comments.raw_body:
Should we be looking at extending the spec? Maybe we should add a "I
have outstanding buffered data" attribute that the client could send to
the server in a CB_GETATTR?
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Scott Mayhew
review_comments.summary: reviewer questioned why the existing change attribute doesn't already account for pending buffered data, pointing out that dirty pages/folios are tracked on the nfs_inode and an elevated change attribute is sent in CB_GETATTR replies
review_comments.raw_body:
I guess I don't understand why the client's usage of the change
attribute doesn't already cover that.

If an application calls write() but not fsync(), close(), etc, and the
background flushers haven't kicked in yet, then the dirty pages/folios
will be tracked on the nfs_inode and the client will send the elevated
change attribute in the CB_GETATTR reply.

What am I missing?

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 2/4] mm: add atomic flush guard for IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Atomic flush guard for IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback to prevent data corruption.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Raised questions about the simplicity of the proposed solution, specifically whether it's sufficient to just wake up the flusher thread after every RWF_DONTCACHE write.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Proposed a simpler approach, suggesting that waking the flusher thread is sufficient for IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback, but noted the need to communicate the higher writeback target.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Apr 06, 2026 at 09:32:58AM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> > And that is called the writeback thread.  So what we should do there
> > is to make sure we queue up writeback on it for each dontcache write.
> > Initially queuing up a wb_writeback_work for each range might be first
> > approximation, although we should probably find a way to just increase
> > a threshold if going down that road.
> > 
> 
> Ok, I like that idea. I'll give that a shot and see how it does. I'll
> note that there is no way to specify an inode or range (yet) in
> wb_writeback_work().
> 
> Do you think it's sufficient to just call something like
> wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi() after every RWF_DONTCACHE write, or should
> I extend wb_writeback_work to allow for doing work on a range within a
> single inode?

I don't think we care about what exact data is written back as this is
not an integrity operation.  So just waking the flusher thread sounds
like the right thing, but we might also need a way to communicate
the higher writeback target.
